These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

...
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

...
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 
...
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **час** | time |
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **чай** | tea |
| **школа** | school |
| **день** | day |
| **слово** | word |
| **мова** | language |

You can also read simple descriptive noun phrases now. Try reading these short combinations aloud to practice fluidity and rhythm. 

- **цей будинок** — this building
- **той хліб** — that bread
- **той парк** — that park
- **ця газета** — this newspaper
- **це слово** — this word
- **та мова** — that language
- **ця школа** — this school

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

...

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: 9 new Cyrillic letters (Б, Д, П, З, Г, Х, Ж, Ш, Ч) and the concept of voiced vs voiceless consonants.
Not covered:
  - Remaining letters Й, Щ, Я, Ю, Є, Ь, Ї, Ц, Ф, Ґ → a1-04

# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>
> You are about to cross a major threshold in your learning journey. By adding these nine consonants, you will know twenty-three letters in total, unlocking the ability to decode nearly all common street signs, menus, and basic words you encounter.

## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for self-correction.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

## Приголосні — Consonants Б, Д, П

- **Це банан?** — Is this a banana?
- **Так, це банан.** — Yes, this is a banana.
- **А це парк?** — And is this a park?
- **Ні, це дім.** — No, this is a home.

These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

### Літера Б
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Б](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg)

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to the previous sound. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк**. This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **банан** | banana |
| **парк** | park |
| **дім** | home |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

- **Де хліб?** — Where is the bread?
- **Хліб тут.** — The bread is here.
- **А газета?** — And the newspaper?
- **Газета там.** — The newspaper is there.

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**. 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

- **Що це?** — What is this?
- **Це шапка.** — This is a hat.
- **А це?** — And this?
- **Це школа.** — This is a school.

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the voiceless partner. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It makes a strong "sh" sound, much like in the English word "ship." However, your tongue should be pulled back slightly compared to the English equivalent for a more authentic tone. You will use it very often, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital sound combinations we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they organize perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

- **Де транспорт?** — Where is the transport?
- **Транспорт там.** — The transport is there.
- **А де пошта?** — And where is the post office?
- **Пошта тут.** — The post office is here.

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants.

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **час** | time |
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **чай** | tea |
| **школа** | school |

You can also read simple descriptive noun phrases now. Try reading these short combinations aloud to practice fluidity and rhythm.

- **цей будинок** — this building
- **той хліб** — that bread
- **той парк** — that park
- **ця газета** — this newspaper

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

- **парк**
- **хліб**
- **шапка**
- **пошта**

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for improving your pronunciation.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

> — Так, це **банан**. (Yes, this is a banana.)
> — А це **парк**? (And is this a park?)
> — Ні, це **дім**. (No, this is a home.)
> — Де **парк**? (Where is the park?)
> — **Парк** там. (The park is there.)
> — Дуже добре. (Very good.)

It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to the previous sound. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк**. This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. [Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **банан** | banana |
| **парк** | park |
| **дім** | home |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**.

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital sound combinations we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they organize perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **час** | time |
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **чай** | tea |
| **школа** | school |
| **день** | day |
| **слово** | word |
| **мова** | language |

You can also read simple descriptive noun phrases now. Try reading these short combinations aloud to practice fluidity and rhythm. 

- **цей будинок** — this building
- **той хліб** — that bread
- **той парк** — that park
- **ця газета** — this newspaper
- **це слово** — this word
- **та мова** — that language
- **ця школа** — this school

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

- **парк**
- **хліб**
- **шапка**
- **пошта**

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for improving your pronunciation.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: 9 new Cyrillic letters (Б, Д, П, З, Г, Х, Ж, Ш, Ч) and the concept of voiced vs voiceless consonants.
Not covered:
  - Remaining letters Й, Щ, Я, Ю, Є, Ь, Ї, Ц, Ф, Ґ → a1-04
-->

# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>
> You are about to cross a major threshold in your learning journey. By adding these nine consonants, you will know twenty-three letters in total, unlocking the ability to decode nearly all common street signs, menus, and basic words you encounter.

## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for improving your pronunciation.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

## Приголосні — Consonants Б, Д, П

> — Це **банан**? (Is this a banana?)
> — Так, це **банан**. (Yes, this is a banana.)
> — А це **парк**? (And is this a park?)
> — Ні, це **дім**. (No, this is a home.)
> — Де **парк**? (Where is the park?)
> — **Парк** там. (The park is there.)
> — Ось **парк**. (Here is the park.)

These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

### Літера Б
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Б](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg)

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to the previous sound. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк**. This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **банан** | banana | **дім** | home |
| **парк** | park | **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building | **день** | day |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**. 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth | **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread | **цей** | this |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital sound combinations we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they organize perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **цей банан тут** | this banana is here |
| **той парк там** | that park is there |
| **цей дім тут** | this home is here |
| **та школа там** | that school is there |
| **ця людина тут** | this person is here |
| **той хліб там** | that bread is there |
| **та шапка тут** | that hat is here |
| **цей зуб там** | this tooth is there |
| **той транспорт тут** | that transport is here |
| **цей чай там** | this tea is there |
| **ця газета тут** | this newspaper is here |
| **ця пошта там** | this post office is there |
| **це слово тут** | this word is here |
| **та мова там** | that language is there |
| **цей день тут** | this day is here |
| **той час там** | that time is there |

We can also use some adverbs to add meaning to our phrases. Read these sentences.

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **він також тут** | he is also here |
| **вона вже там** | she is already there |
| **ми теж тут** | we are also here |
| **ви ще там** | you are still there |
| **вони тільки тут** | they are only here |
| **я також там** | I am also there |
| **ти вже тут** | you are already here |
| **він дуже там** | he is very much there |
| **вона теж тут** | she is also here |
| **ми тільки там** | we are only there |

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **парк** | park |
| **хліб** | bread |
| **шапка** | hat |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **зуб** | tooth |

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: 9 new Cyrillic letters (Б, Д, П, З, Г, Х, Ж, Ш, Ч) and the concept of voiced vs voiceless consonants.
Not covered:
  - Remaining letters Й, Щ, Я, Ю, Є, Ь, Ї, Ц, Ф, Ґ → a1-04
-->

# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>
> You are about to cross a major threshold in your learning journey. By adding these nine consonants, you will know twenty-three letters in total, unlocking the ability to decode nearly all common street signs, menus, and basic words you encounter.

## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for improving your pronunciation.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

## Приголосні — Consonants Б, Д, П

> — Це **банан**? (Is this a banana?)
> — Так, це **банан**. (Yes, this is a banana.)
> — А це **парк**? (And is this a park?)
> — Ні, це **дім**. (No, this is a home.)
> — Де **парк**? (Where is the park?)
> — **Парк** там. (The park is there.)
> — Дуже добре. (Very good.)

These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

### Літера Б
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Б](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg)

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to the previous sound. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк**. This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **банан** | banana |
| **парк** | park |
| **дім** | home |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**. 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation |
|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital sound combinations we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they organize perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **час** | time |
| **зуб** | tooth |
| **чай** | tea |
| **школа** | school |
| **день** | day |
| **слово** | word |
| **мова** | language |

You can also read simple descriptive noun phrases now. Try reading these short combinations aloud to practice fluidity and rhythm. 

- **цей будинок** — this building
- **той хліб** — that bread
- **той парк** — that park
- **ця газета** — this newspaper
- **це слово** — this word
- **та мова** — that language
- **ця школа** — this school

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

- **парк**
- **хліб**
- **шапка**
- **пошта**

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: 9 new Cyrillic letters (Б, Д, П, З, Г, Х, Ж, Ш, Ч) and the concept of voiced vs voiceless consonants.
Not covered:
  - Remaining letters Й, Щ, Я, Ю, Є, Ь, Ї, Ц, Ф, Ґ → a1-04
-->

# The Cyrillic Code III

> **Why does this matter?**
>
> You are about to cross a major threshold in your learning journey. By adding these nine consonants, you will know twenty-three letters in total, unlocking the ability to decode nearly all common street signs, menus, and basic words you encounter.

## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome to your third step in cracking the Cyrillic alphabet. You have already mastered the vowels and a solid foundation of essential consonants. Now, we are expanding your decodability even further, allowing you to read real-world vocabulary with much greater confidence. 

In this lesson, we will introduce nine new consonants. This brings your total to twenty-three letters. You will quickly notice that the more letters you learn, the faster you can recognize international words and essential everyday vocabulary. This progression transforms abstract symbols into meaningful language that you can immediately apply.

A key concept we will explore today is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. Ukrainian consonants often come in matched pairs. One sound vibrates in your throat, which is called a voiced consonant. The other sound uses only air and no vibration, which is called a voiceless consonant. Understanding this mechanical difference is the secret to developing a natural and authentic accent. 

> [!tip]
> **Check your vibration**
> Place your hand lightly on your throat. When you make a voiced sound, you will feel a distinct buzz. When you make a voiceless sound, you will only feel air moving. This physical feedback is a powerful tool for improving your pronunciation.

Let us dive into the first group of new letters and see how many words you can already read.

## Приголосні — Consonants Б, Д, П

> — Це **банан**? (Is this a banana?)
> — Так, це **банан**. (Yes, this is a banana.)
> — А це **парк**? (And is this a park?)
> — Ні, це **дім**. (No, this is a home.)
> — Де **парк**? (Where is the park?)
> — **Парк** там. (The park is there.)
> — Ось **парк**. (Here is the park.)

These three consonants are extremely common and will help you form many essential words used in daily life. Let us look at them closely and understand their unique characteristics.

The letter **Б** is a voiced consonant. Visually, it looks a bit like the number six, but it sounds exactly like the English equivalent. It is a foundational sound that you will encounter frequently. It is the first letter in the word **банан**. Watch the pronunciation video below to hear it clearly and practice repeating it aloud.

### Літера Б
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Б](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg)

The letter **П** is the voiceless partner to the previous sound. Be careful not to confuse it with the Greek symbol Pi or the letter for the "R" sound you learned previously. The Ukrainian **П** is crisp and completely unaspirated, meaning there is absolutely no puff of air when you say it. It starts the word **парк**. This lack of aspiration is crucial for sounding like a native speaker.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **банан** | banana | **дім** | home |
| **парк** | park | **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building | **день** | day |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**. 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth | **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread | **цей** | this |

> [!culture]
> **The Importance of Bread**
> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

We have extensively mentioned paired sounds throughout this lesson. This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

Let us summarize the vital sound combinations we have learned so far. Notice how elegantly they organize perfectly within the phonetic system.

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> [!did-you-know]
> **Visual Mnemonics**
> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

## Практика читання — Reading Practice

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

Now that you have twenty-three robust letters securely in your mental toolkit, you can confidently read a vast amount of practical vocabulary. This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **цей банан тут** | this banana is here |
| **той парк там** | that park is there |
| **цей дім тут** | this home is here |
| **та школа там** | that school is there |
| **ця людина тут** | this person is here |
| **той хліб там** | that bread is there |
| **та шапка тут** | that hat is here |
| **цей зуб там** | this tooth is there |
| **той транспорт тут** | that transport is here |
| **цей чай там** | this tea is there |
| **ця газета тут** | this newspaper is here |
| **ця пошта там** | this post office is there |
| **це слово тут** | this word is here |
| **та мова там** | that language is there |
| **цей день тут** | this day is here |
| **той час там** | that time is there |

We can also use some adverbs to add meaning to our phrases. Read these sentences.

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **він також тут** | he is also here |
| **вона вже там** | she is already there |
| **ми теж тут** | we are also here |
| **ви ще там** | you are still there |
| **вони тільки тут** | they are only here |
| **я також там** | I am also there |
| **ти вже тут** | you are already here |
| **він дуже там** | he is very much there |
| **вона теж тут** | she is also here |
| **ми тільки там** | we are only there |

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

## Підсумок — Summary

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

Let us perform a final, comprehensive decodability check. Can you read and instantly translate the following essential words?

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
| **парк** | park |
| **хліб** | bread |
| **шапка** | hat |
| **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building |
| **газета** | newspaper |
| **транспорт** | transport |
| **зуб** | tooth |

In the next and final module of this foundational series, we will introduce the remaining ten characters, including the soft vowels and special pronunciation symbols, successfully completing your journey to full alphabet mastery.

### Літера П
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — П](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y)

The letter **Д** is a voiced consonant. Its shape resembles a little house resting securely on a solid foundation. It sounds similar to the English equivalent, but there is a subtle difference in tongue placement. The tip of your tongue should touch your upper teeth rather than the roof of your mouth. You will see it in the high-frequency word **дім**.

### Літера Д
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Д](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48)

Let us practice reading some words using these letters. Read them aloud slowly, and pay close attention to the letters you already know from previous lessons.

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **банан** | banana | **дім** | home |
| **парк** | park | **пошта** | post office |
| **будинок** | building | **день** | day |

> [!warning]
> **False Friends**
> Remember that the letter you learned previously that looks like a Latin "P" actually makes the "R" sound. The new symbol makes the "P" sound. Contrast a word like **парк** with words containing the "R" sound to solidify this distinction in your memory.

## Приголосні — Consonants З, Г, Х

> — Де **хліб**? (Where is the bread?)
> — **Хліб** тут. (The bread is here.)
> — А **газета**? (And the newspaper?)
> — **Газета** там. (The newspaper is there.)
> — Де **будинок**? (Where is the building?)
> — Цей **будинок** тут. (This building is here.)

Now we will look at three more consonants that expand your range of expression. These include a sharp hissing sound and two sounds that are formed deeper in your mouth and throat. Mastering these will significantly improve your overall pronunciation.

The letter **З** is a voiced consonant. It looks strikingly similar to the number three. It makes a clear buzzing sound, completely identical to the first sound in the English word "zebra." We find it in the everyday word **зуб**. Focus on sustaining the vibration when you pronounce it.

### Літера З
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — З](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A)

The letter **Г** is voiced, but it might be the trickiest character in this group for English speakers. It is a soft, throaty sound. It is definitively not a hard sound like in the English word "go." Instead, it sounds like a voiced glottal fricative, almost like a heavy, vibrating sigh. You can practice it with the word **газета**.

### Літера Г
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Г](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU)

The letter **Х** is the voiceless counterpart to the sound above. Visually, it looks exactly like the Latin "X," but it absolutely does not make a "ks" sound. Instead, it sounds like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch" or the German "Bach." A culturally important word starting with this letter is **хліб**. 

### Літера Х
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Х](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc)

Let us look at these letters in context. Notice how they combine smoothly with the vowels you have already mastered to form recognizable vocabulary. 

| Word | Translation | Word | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **зуб** | tooth | **газета** | newspaper |
| **хліб** | bread | **цей** | this |

> The word **хліб** is deeply foundational and revered in Ukrainian culture. Bread is a timeless symbol of hospitality, life, community, and prosperity. Conveniently, you can now read this essential word completely using the letters you have learned!

## Дзвінкі та глухі — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs

> — Що це? (What is this?)
> — Це **шапка**. (This is a hat.)
> — А це? (And this?)
> — Це **школа**. (This is a school.)
> — Де **школа**? (Where is the school?)
> — Та **школа** там. (That school is there.)

This phonetic concept means that your mouth, lips, and tongue are in the exact same physical position, but one variant uses active vocal cord vibration while the other uses only a stream of air. We will now learn three distinctive symbols collectively called "hushers" that perfectly demonstrate this system.

The letter **Ж** is voiced. Visually, it looks quite a bit like a six-legged beetle, and appropriately, it makes a rich buzzing sound similar to a flying insect. The sound is very close to the middle consonant in the English word "measure." Ensure your lips are slightly rounded when you produce it.

### Літера Ж
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ж](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM)

The letter **Ш** is the unvoiced companion. It looks somewhat like a pitchfork pointing squarely upwards. It produces a strong hissing noise, much like the English "sh" in "ship," but your tongue must be pulled back slightly for a more authentic tone. It appears very frequently, such as in the word **школа**.

### Літера Ш
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ш](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY)

The letter **Ч** is also voiceless. It visually resembles an upside-down chair or a handwritten number four. It makes a sharp "ch" sound, similar to the beginning of the English word "church." It appears frequently in basic vocabulary, such as in the word **час** and the universally recognized word **чай**. 

### Літера Ч
[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA)

- **Б** and **П** form a matched pair.
- **Д** goes with the letter for the "T" sound.
- **З** links with the letter for the "S" sound.
- **Ж** and **Ш** are direct partners.

> Culturally, Ukrainian children are very often taught that the letter for the buzzing sound looks exactly like a little bug, and the sound it makes mimics the loud buzzing of an insect. This is a very authentic, widely used, and helpful memory trick!

> — Де **транспорт**? (Where is the transport?)
> — **Транспорт** там. (The transport is there.)
> — А де **пошта**? (And where is the post office?)
> — **Пошта** тут. (The post office is here.)
> — Де **людина**? (Where is the person?)
> — **Людина** там. (The person is there.)

This exciting stage includes many international words that you will immediately recognize once you sound out the letters sequentially. Knowing these words unlocks everyday interactions in the real world. 

Let us practice reading some new words together. Take your time, focus on accuracy, and sound out each individual letter carefully. Remember the consonant pairings we discussed and consciously try to feel the vibration in your throat for the resonant consonants. You can read all of these!

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **цей банан тут** | this banana is here |
| **той парк там** | that park is there |
| **цей дім тут** | this home is here |
| **та школа там** | that school is there |
| **ця людина тут** | this person is here |
| **той хліб там** | that bread is there |
| **та шапка тут** | that hat is here |
| **цей зуб там** | this tooth is there |
| **той транспорт тут** | that transport is here |
| **цей чай там** | this tea is there |
| **ця газета тут** | this newspaper is here |
| **ця пошта там** | this post office is there |
| **це слово тут** | this word is here |
| **та мова там** | that language is there |
| **цей день тут** | this day is here |
| **той час там** | that time is there |

We can also use some adverbs to add meaning to our phrases. Read these sentences.

| Ukrainian Phrase | Translation |
|---|---|
| **він також тут** | he is also here |
| **вона вже там** | she is already there |
| **ми теж тут** | we are also here |
| **ви ще там** | you are still there |
| **вони тільки тут** | they are only here |
| **я також там** | I am also there |
| **ти вже тут** | you are already here |
| **він дуже там** | he is very much there |
| **вона теж тут** | she is also here |
| **ми тільки там** | we are only there |

Notice exactly how the words feel physically in your mouth as you transition between sounds. The clear distinction between a soft throat sound and a sharp hissing sound is precisely what gives the language its beautifully unique rhythm. You are actively building essential muscle memory every single time you read aloud. Keep practicing these phrases until they feel entirely natural and effortless. You will find that recognizing these patterns accelerates your reading speed significantly.

You have made absolutely incredible progress in this session. You can now reliably recognize and accurately pronounce twenty-three of the thirty-three letters in the entire alphabet. This is a massive milestone in your language acquisition journey.

This impressive collection includes six foundational vowels and seventeen versatile consonants. You also now deeply understand the crucial mechanical difference between sounds that vibrate and sounds that only use a stream of air. This mechanical understanding will significantly and permanently improve your accent.

| Ukrainian | Translation |
|---|---|
